book_summary: Joshua has chapters 1-16 complete in restored/source-audited draft and mystical companion layers. The book now covers Joshua's succession, Rahab, Jordan crossing, memorial stones, Passover, Jericho, Achan, Ai, the Gibeonite survival treaty and forced labor, southern and northern mass-destruction campaigns, the sun-and-moon poem, the thirty-one-kings list, Joshua's old age and unconquered lands, eastern and western allotments, Caleb's Hebron claim, Achsah's request for springs, Judah's extensive city register, continuing Jebusite and Philistine presence, and Ephraim's coercive rule over Canaanites in Gezer. Safeguards reject colonial conquest, genocide, child killing, anti-sex-worker stigma, forced genital modification, leader worship, collective punishment, celestial pseudoscience, prisoner humiliation, animal cruelty, Divine-hardening excuses, slavery, forced labor, modern border weaponization, ageism, ableism, patriarchal treatment of women as rewards, and administrative erasure of living communities.

chapters.notes: Gibeonite deception as survival under extermination threat, failed inquiry, binding oath, rescue from massacre, and hereditary wood-and-water forced labor under anti-slavery safeguards.
Defense of Gibeon, five-king coalition, hail, sun-and-moon poem from Scroll of Jashar, captive humiliation and execution, and repeated no-survivor southern-city destruction.
Northern coalition, chariots, horse hamstringing, Hazor burning, prolonged war, no-survivor formulas, hardening theology, Anakim, and victor-defined rest.
East- and west-Jordan defeated-kings register, thirty-one total, uncertain place names, and administrative compression of people and violence into counted rulers.
Joshua's old age, much land remaining, living Philistine/Sidonian/Geshurite/Maacathite populations, east-Jordan allotments, Balaam death notice, and Levite nonterritorial inheritance.
Shared allocation leadership, Caleb's Kenizzite identity, forty-five-year promise, individual vigor at eighty-five, Hebron/Anakim claim, and age/ability safeguards.
Judah boundaries and cities, Caleb/Othniel conquest, Achsah as marriage reward who directly secures upper and lower springs, Philistine allotment tension, and continuing Jebusite Jerusalem.
Joseph/Ephraim boundary, enclave cities within Manasseh, continuing Canaanite presence in Gezer, and forced labor under tribute rather than equal coexistence.

translation_notes: Joshua remains a conquest narrative whose courage, memory, covenant, and public-Torah strands cannot ethically sanitize its massacres and displacement.
Rahab and the Gibeonites demonstrate threatened communities exercising survival intelligence under approaching military destruction.
Protection agreements remain binding even when powerful parties later resent the conditions under which they were made.
Gibeonite survival through hereditary wood-and-water labor is forced subordination, not benevolent inclusion.
The sun-and-moon text is ancient poetry cited from the lost Scroll of Jashar and must not be forced into modern astronomical claims.
Feet on captive necks, displayed bodies, no-survivor formulas, city burning, and every-breath destruction are categorically non-reenactable.
Horse hamstringing is animal harm even when narrated as demilitarization of chariot power.
Divine-hardening language must never excuse refusal of diplomacy or make victims responsible for genocide.
A king list can administratively erase civilians and communities by turning conquest into names and totals.
The book itself admits much land remained and that Jebusite, Philistine, Geshurite, Maacathite, Canaanite, and other communities continued living within or beside assigned territories.
Allotment maps describe ancient claims and administrative ideals, not modern borders, title deeds, or permission to displace current populations.
Joshua's aging redistributes unfinished work; leaders do not lose dignity because one lifetime cannot complete a collective project.
Caleb's exceptional vigor must not shame elders who are frail, ill, disabled, or dependent.
Achsah is situated in a patriarchal marriage-prize system but acts directly to secure water; land without life-sustaining resources is not meaningful inheritance.
Gezer's Canaanites survive but are placed under forced labor and tribute; survival without freedom is not justice.
Mystical companion layers remain interpretive and never replace restored/source-audited text.
source_witness_audit_flags: Joshua 9 Gibeonite identity, moldy-provision vocabulary, treaty oath, curse formula, and woodcutter/water-drawer status require treaty, slavery, and reception-history audit.

Joshua 10 Scroll of Jashar citation, sun/moon verbs, long-day interpretation, hail tradition, campaign geography, and five-king execution require textual, astronomical-history, and archaeological audit.
Joshua 11 Hazor burning strata, Merom geography, chariot warfare, horse-hamstringing, hardening language, and land-rest summary require archaeological and ethical audit.
Joshua 12 place identifications, unnarrated kings, Goiim/Gilgal, Lasharon, and thirty-one total require textual-witness and historical-geography audit.
Joshua 13 Philistine editorial horizon, remaining-land list, east-Jordan borders, Balaam death cross-reference, and Geshurite/Maacathite continuity require compositional and geographic audit.
Joshua 14 lot procedure, Caleb's Kenizzite identity, age chronology, Anakim tradition, and Hebron/Kiriath-arba require textual and archaeological audit.
Joshua 15 boundary sites, city totals, Septuagint additions near verse 59, Achsah/Othniel grammar, and continuing Jebusite presence require textual-witness, gender, and geographic audit.
Joshua 16 Bethel/Luz relationship, enclave cities, Gezer archaeology, and forced-labor terminology require textual, archaeological, and labor-history audit.
next_actions: Finish Joshua in one large batch with chapters 17-24: Manasseh and Zelophehad's daughters, Joseph-tribe land complaint, Shiloh survey and remaining allotments, cities of refuge, Levite cities, the east-Jordan altar dispute and successful de-escalation, Joshua's farewell warnings, Shechem covenant renewal, 'choose this day,' ancestral-god traditions, covenant stone, Joshua and Eleazar deaths, and Joseph's burial—with anti-colonial, anti-slavery, anti-war, intertribal de-escalation, gender-inheritance, anti-idolatry-persecution, and grief/succession safeguards.
